title: Oshikango

infobox.value: [UTC+1](/wiki/UTC%2B1 "UTC+1") ([South African Standard Time](/wiki/South_African_Standard_Time "South African Standard Time"))

is a former village in northern [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia") and since 2004 part of the town of [Helao Nafidi](/wiki/Helao_Nafidi "Helao Nafidi"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) although it still maintained its own village council for a number of years. *Oshikango* is still the name of the border post with [Angola](/wiki/Angola "Angola") and the [electoral constituency](/wiki/Oshikango_Constituency "Oshikango Constituency") for this suburb. It is estimated to have grown from "a tiny cluster of [shebeens](/wiki/Shebeen "Shebeen") around an open market into a thriving boomtown with around 5,000 to 8,000 inhabitants over a period of 10 years".[[2]](#cite_note-Niikondo-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Shivute, Oswald (26 September 2006). ["Transition to modern town life tough for villagers"](http://www.namibian.com.na/index.php?id=28&tx_ttnews%5Btt_news%5D=30318&no_cache=1). [The Namibian](/wiki/The_Namibian "The Namibian").

Niikondo, Andrew (August 2010). ["Potential of friction in border town developments: A case study of Oshikango in the Helao Nafidi Town Council, Northern Namibia"](http://www.managers.org.cn/mag/doc/ucman201008/ucman20100802.pdf) (PDF). *Journal of US-China Public Administration*. **7** (8). [ISSN](/wiki/ISSN_(identifier) "ISSN (identifier)") [1548-6591](https://search.worldcat.org/issn/1548-6591).[*[permanent dead link](/wiki/Wikipedia:Link_rot "Wikipedia:Link rot")*]

Terblanché, Niël (1 September 2008). ["Oshikango se dorpsraad dalk voor hof oor grond"](https://web.archive.org/web/20160304073949/http://www.republikein.com.na/politiek-en-nasionale/oshikango-se-dorpsraad-dalk-voor-hof-oor-grond.72350.php) [Oshikango's village council taken to court over land] (in Afrikaans). [Die Republikein](/wiki/Die_Republikein "Die Republikein"). Archived from [the original](http://www.republikein.com.na/politiek-en-nasionale/oshikango-se-dorpsraad-dalk-voor-hof-oor-grond.72350.php) on 4 March 2016. Retrieved 17 December 2010.

["Chinese rails for Oshikango railway"](https://web.archive.org/web/20110607201940/http://www.namibian.com.na/news-articles/national/full-story/archive/2009/november/article/chinese-rails-for-oshikango-railway/). *[The Namibian](/wiki/The_Namibian "The Namibian")*. 13 November 2009. Archived from [the original](http://www.namibian.com.na/news-articles/national/full-story/archive/2009/november/article/chinese-rails-for-oshikango-railway/) on 7 June 2011.
["Govt extends rail link into Angola"](http://www.namibian.com.na/index.php?id=28&tx_ttnews%5Btt_news%5D=49977&no_cache=1). *[The Namibian](/wiki/The_Namibian "The Namibian")*. 6 October 2008.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Not to be confused with [Otjikango](/wiki/Otjikango "Otjikango").

Village in Ohangwena Region, Namibia

| Oshikango | |
| --- | --- |
| village | |

| Coordinates: [17°24′S 15°53′E﻿ / ﻿17.400°S 15.883°E﻿ / -17.400; 15.883](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Oshikango&params=17_24_S_15_53_E_region:NA_type:city) | |
| Country | [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia") |
| [Region](/wiki/Regions_of_Namibia "Regions of Namibia") | [Ohangwena Region](/wiki/Ohangwena_Region "Ohangwena Region") |
| [Region](/wiki/Constituencies_of_Namibia "Constituencies of Namibia") | [Oshikango Constituency](/wiki/Oshikango_Constituency "Oshikango Constituency") |
| Elevation | 3,606 ft (1,099 m) |
| [Time zone](/wiki/Time_zone "Time zone") | [UTC+1](/wiki/UTC%2B1 "UTC+1") ([South African Standard Time](/wiki/South_African_Standard_Time "South African Standard Time")) |

**Oshikango** is a former village in northern [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia") and since 2004 part of the town of [Helao Nafidi](/wiki/Helao_Nafidi "Helao Nafidi"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) although it still maintained its own village council for a number of years. *Oshikango* is still the name of the border post with [Angola](/wiki/Angola "Angola") and the [electoral constituency](/wiki/Oshikango_Constituency "Oshikango Constituency") for this suburb. It is estimated to have grown from "a tiny cluster of [shebeens](/wiki/Shebeen "Shebeen") around an open market into a thriving boomtown with around 5,000 to 8,000 inhabitants over a period of 10 years".[[2]](#cite_note-Niikondo-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oshikango&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Oshikango area was heavily affected by the 1966 to 1989 [South African Border War](/wiki/South_African_Border_War "South African Border War") between [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and its allied forces (mainly [UNITA](/wiki/UNITA "UNITA")) and the Angolan government and the [South West Africa People's Organisation](/wiki/South_West_Africa_People%27s_Organisation "South West Africa People's Organisation") (SWAPO). The war ended with South Africa agreeing to Namibian independence. In 1996 Oshikango, along with many other settlements in the area, was proclaimed a village with the aim of increasing border trade.[[4]](#cite_note-TradeShow-4)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oshikango&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Economy")]

The border post between Namibia and Angola has brought business opportunities to Oshikango and the surrounding area. With the help of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") an [Export Processing Zone](/wiki/Export_Processing_Zone "Export Processing Zone") consisting of 14 warehouses was established there. The nearby village of [Omafo](/w/index.php?title=Omafo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Omafo (page does not exist)"), now a suburb of Helao Nafidi, hosts an annual trade show.[[4]](#cite_note-TradeShow-4)

The parallel prevalence of land owned by the village of Oshikango, the town of [Helao Nafidi](/wiki/Helao_Nafidi "Helao Nafidi"), and land under [traditional jurisdiction](/wiki/Tribal_chief "Tribal chief") has led to uncertainties about ownership that had to be settled in court.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

There are some manufacturing companies in Oshikango, for example Fatima Plastic which donated [Engela](/wiki/Engela "Engela") hospital the cost of building a bridge after a flood. Chicco, a building material wholesaler, has also set up shop in Oshikango, and there are also large retailers like [Pick n Pay](/wiki/Pick_n_Pay_Stores "Pick n Pay Stores") and Fysal Fresh Produce. Most villagers however, still mainly survive by agriculture and face the risk of periodic [drought](/wiki/Drought "Drought").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oshikango&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/23022011068.jpg/250px-23022011068.jpg)](/wiki/File:23022011068.jpg)

Street in Oshikango

In mid-2005, the second stage of the new Northern Railway between Oshikango and [Oshivelo](/wiki/Oshivelo "Oshivelo") began construction. By mid-2006, it had reached [Ondangwa](/wiki/Ondangwa "Ondangwa"). A train service known as the *[Omugulugwombashe Star](/wiki/Omugulugwombashe#Omugulugwombashe_Star "Omugulugwombashe")* traveled weekly on this track until the locomotives broke down after a few rounds of service.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 2008, a short extension across the border was proposed to bypass the congested border post.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The next station to the south is [Ohangwena](/wiki/Ohangwena "Ohangwena").

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oshikango&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Life")]

Oshikango is considered the business hub of the North in Namibia. Many foreign as well as local investors have chosen Oshikango as their stepping-stone for exports into Angola. A governing body dubbed the Oshikango Business Association, in conjunction with Local Government as well as the NCCI ([Namibia Chamber of Commerce and Industry](/wiki/Namibia_Chamber_of_Commerce_and_Industry "Namibia Chamber of Commerce and Industry")) regulate all business related matters in the area.

## External links

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oshikango&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: External links")]

* [Map of Oshikango](http://www.maplandia.com/namibia/ohangwena/oshikango/)
